question:
For positive integers $m$ and $n$, let $f(m,n)$ denote the number of
$n$-tuples $(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n)$ of integers such that
$|x_1| + |x_2| + \cdots + |x_n| \leq m$.
Show that $f(m,n) = f(n,m)$.
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Define $f(m,n,k)$ as the number of $n$-tuples $(x_1, x_2,\dots,x_n)$
of integers such that $|x_1| + \cdots + |x_n| \leq m$ and exactly
$k$ of $x_1, \dots, x_n$ are nonzero. To choose such a tuple, we may choose
the $k$ nonzero positions, the signs of those $k$ numbers, and
then an ordered $k$-tuple of positive integers with sum $\leq m$.
There are $\binom{n}{k}$ options for the first choice, and $2^k$ for the
second. As for the third, we have
$\binom{m}{k}$ options by a ``stars and bars'' argument: depict the
$k$-tuple by drawing a number of stars for each term, separated by bars,
and adding stars at the end to get a total of $m$ stars. Then each tuple
corresponds to placing $k$ bars, each in a different position behind one
of the $m$ fixed stars.

We conclude that
\[
f(m,n,k) = 2^k\binom{m}{k} \binom{n}{k} = f(n,m,k);
\]
summing over $k$ gives $f(m,n) = f(n,m)$. (One may also extract easily a
bijective interpretation of the equality.)

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Kuperberg)
It will be convenient to extend the definition of $f(m,n)$ to $m,n \geq 0$,
in which case we have $f(0,m) = f(n,0) = 1$.

Let $S_{m,n}$ be the set of $n$-tuples $(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ of integers
such that $|x_1| + \cdots + |x_n| \leq m$. Then elements of $S_{m,n}$
can be classified into three types. Tuples with $|x_1| + \cdots + |x_n| < m$
also belong to $S_{m-1,n}$. Tuples with $|x_1| + \cdots + |x_n| = m$
and $x_n \geq 0$ correspond to elements of $S_{m,n-1}$ by dropping $x_n$.
Tuples with $|x_1| + \cdots + |x_n| = m$ and $x_n < 0$ correspond to
elements of $S_{m-1,n-1}$ by dropping $x_n$. It follows that
\[
f(m,n) = f(m-1,n) + f(m,n-1) + f(m-1,n-1),
\]
so $f$ satisfies a symmetric recurrence with symmetric boundary conditions
$f(0,m) = f(n,0) = 1$. Hence $f$ is symmetric.

\def\ZZ{\mathbb{Z}}
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Greg Martin)
As in the second solution,
it is convenient to allow $f(m,0)=f(0,n)=1$. Define the generating function
\[
G(x,y) = \sum_{m=0}^\infty \sum_{n=0}^\infty f(m,n) x^m y^n.
\]
As equalities of formal power series (or convergent series on,
say, the region $|x|,|y|<\frac13$), we have
\begin{align*}
G(x,y) &= \sum_{m\ge0} \sum_{n\ge0} x^m y^n \sum_{\substack{k_1,\,\dots,\,k_n \in \ZZ \\ |k_1| + \cdots + |k_n| \le m}} 1 \\
&= \sum_{n\ge0} y^n \sum_{k_1,\,\dots,\,k_n \in \ZZ} \sum_{m\ge|k_1| + \cdots + |k_n|} x^m \\
&= \sum_{n\ge0} y^n \sum_{k_1,\,\dots,\,k_n \in \ZZ} \frac{x^{|k_1| + \cdots + |k_n|}}{1-x} \\
&= \frac1{1-x} \sum_{n\ge0} y^n \bigg( \sum_{k\in\ZZ} x^{|k|} \bigg)^n \\
&= \frac1{1-x} \sum_{n\ge0} y^n \bigg( \frac{1+x}{1-x} \bigg)^n \\
&= \frac1{1-x} \cdot \frac1{1-y(1+x)/(1-x)} \\
&= \frac1{1-x-y-xy}.
\end{align*}
Since $G(x,y) = G(y,x)$, it follows that $f(m,n) = f(n,m)$ for all $m,n\ge0$.

descriptive_long.question:
For positive integers $boundm$ and $tuplelen$, let $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen)$ denote the number of
$tuplelen$-tuples $(firstxvar,x_2,\dots,nthxvar)$ of integers such that
$|firstxvar| + |x_2| + \cdots + |nthxvar| \leq boundm$.
Show that $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen) = tuplecount(tuplelen,boundm)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Define $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen,nonzerocount)$ as the number of $tuplelen$-tuples $(firstxvar, x_2,\dots,nthxvar)$
of integers such that $|firstxvar| + \cdots + |nthxvar| \leq boundm$ and exactly
$nonzerocount$ of $firstxvar, \dots, nthxvar$ are nonzero. To choose such a tuple, we may choose
the $nonzerocount$ nonzero positions, the signs of those $nonzerocount$ numbers, and
then an ordered $nonzerocount$-tuple of positive integers with sum $\leq boundm$.
There are $\binom{tuplelen}{nonzerocount}$ options for the first choice, and $2^{nonzerocount}$ for the
second. As for the third, we have
$\binom{boundm}{nonzerocount}$ options by a ``stars and bars'' argument: depict the
$nonzerocount$-tuple by drawing a number of stars for each term, separated by bars,
and adding stars at the end to get a total of $boundm$ stars. Then each tuple
corresponds to placing $nonzerocount$ bars, each in a different position behind one
of the $boundm$ fixed stars.

We conclude that
\[
tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen,nonzerocount) = 2^{nonzerocount}\binom{boundm}{nonzerocount} \binom{tuplelen}{nonzerocount} = tuplecount(tuplelen,boundm,nonzerocount);
\]
summing over $nonzerocount$ gives $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen) = tuplecount(tuplelen,boundm)$. (One may also extract easily a
bijective interpretation of the equality.)

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Kuperberg)
It will be convenient to extend the definition of $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen)$ to $boundm,tuplelen \geq 0$,
in which case we have $tuplecount(0,boundm) = tuplecount(tuplelen,0) = 1$.

Let $tuplecountset_{boundm,tuplelen}$ be the set of $tuplelen$-tuples $(firstxvar, \dots, nthxvar)$ of integers
such that $|firstxvar| + \cdots + |nthxvar| \leq boundm$. Then elements of $tuplecountset_{boundm,tuplelen}$
can be classified into three types. Tuples with $|firstxvar| + \cdots + |nthxvar| < boundm$
also belong to $tuplecountset_{boundm-1,tuplelen}$. Tuples with $|firstxvar| + \cdots + |nthxvar| = boundm$
and $nthxvar \geq 0$ correspond to elements of $tuplecountset_{boundm,tuplelen-1}$ by dropping $nthxvar$.
Tuples with $|firstxvar| + \cdots + |nthxvar| = boundm$ and $nthxvar < 0$ correspond to
elements of $tuplecountset_{boundm-1,tuplelen-1}$ by dropping $nthxvar$. It follows that
\[
tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen) = tuplecount(boundm-1,tuplelen) + tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen-1) + tuplecount(boundm-1,tuplelen-1),
\]
so $tuplecount$ satisfies a symmetric recurrence with symmetric boundary conditions
$tuplecount(0,boundm) = tuplecount(tuplelen,0) = 1$. Hence $tuplecount$ is symmetric.

\def\ZZ{\mathbb{Z}}
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Greg Martin)
As in the second solution,
it is convenient to allow $tuplecount(boundm,0)=tuplecount(0,tuplelen)=1$. Define the generating function
\[
genfunct(seriesvarx,seriesvary) = \sum_{boundm=0}^{\infty} \sum_{tuplelen=0}^{\infty} tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen) seriesvarx^{boundm} seriesvary^{tuplelen}.
\]
As equalities of formal power series (or convergent series on,
say, the region $|seriesvarx|,|seriesvary|<\frac13$), we have
\begin{align*}
genfunct(seriesvarx,seriesvary) &= \sum_{boundm\ge0} \sum_{tuplelen\ge0} seriesvarx^{boundm} seriesvary^{tuplelen} \sum_{\substack{firstkvar,\,\dots,\,nthkvar \in \ZZ \\ |firstkvar| + \cdots + |nthkvar| \le boundm}} 1 \\
&= \sum_{tuplelen\ge0} seriesvary^{tuplelen} \sum_{firstkvar,\,\dots,\,nthkvar \in \ZZ} \sum_{boundm\ge|firstkvar| + \cdots + |nthkvar|} seriesvarx^{boundm} \\
&= \sum_{tuplelen\ge0} seriesvary^{tuplelen} \sum_{firstkvar,\,\dots,\,nthkvar \in \ZZ} \frac{seriesvarx^{|firstkvar| + \cdots + |nthkvar|}}{1-seriesvarx} \\
&= \frac1{1-seriesvarx} \sum_{tuplelen\ge0} seriesvary^{tuplelen} \bigg( \sum_{nonzerocount\in\ZZ} seriesvarx^{|nonzerocount|} \bigg)^{\!tuplelen} \\
&= \frac1{1-seriesvarx} \sum_{tuplelen\ge0} seriesvary^{tuplelen} \bigg( \frac{1+seriesvarx}{1-seriesvarx} \bigg)^{\!tuplelen} \\
&= \frac1{1-seriesvarx} \cdot \frac1{1-seriesvary(1+seriesvarx)/(1-seriesvarx)} \\
&= \frac1{1-seriesvarx-seriesvary-seriesvarx seriesvary}.
\end{align*}
Since $genfunct(seriesvarx,seriesvary) = genfunct(seriesvary,seriesvarx)$, it follows that $tuplecount(boundm,tuplelen) = tuplecount(tuplelen,boundm)$ for all $boundm,tuplelen\ge0$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: For positive integers starfruits and riverbanks, let hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) denote the number of riverbanks-tuples (quasarbeam,x_2,\dots,glacierbay) of integers such that |quasarbeam| + |x_2| + \cdots + |glacierbay| \leq starfruits. Show that hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) = hummingbird(riverbanks,starfruits).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Define $hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks,stormcloud)$ as the number of riverbanks-tuples $(quasarbeam, x_2,\dots,glacierbay)$
of integers such that $|quasarbeam| + \cdots + |glacierbay| \leq starfruits$ and exactly
$stormcloud$ of $quasarbeam, \dots, glacierbay$ are nonzero. To choose such a tuple, we may choose
the $stormcloud$ nonzero positions, the signs of those $stormcloud$ numbers, and
then an ordered $stormcloud$-tuple of positive integers with sum $\leq starfruits$.
There are $\binom{riverbanks}{stormcloud}$ options for the first choice, and $2^{stormcloud}$ for the
second. As for the third, we have
$\binom{starfruits}{stormcloud}$ options by a ``stars and bars'' argument: depict the
$stormcloud$-tuple by drawing a number of stars for each term, separated by bars,
and adding stars at the end to get a total of $starfruits$ stars. Then each tuple
corresponds to placing $stormcloud$ bars, each in a different position behind one
of the $starfruits$ fixed stars.

We conclude that
\[
hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks,stormcloud) = 2^{stormcloud}\binom{starfruits}{stormcloud} \binom{riverbanks}{stormcloud} = hummingbird(riverbanks,starfruits,stormcloud);
\]
summing over $stormcloud$ gives $hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) = hummingbird(riverbanks,starfruits)$. (One may also extract easily a
bijective interpretation of the equality.)

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Kuperberg)
It will be convenient to extend the definition of $hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks)$ to $starfruits,riverbanks \geq 0$,
in which case we have $hummingbird(0,starfruits) = hummingbird(riverbanks,0) = 1$.

Let $waterlilies$ be the set of riverbanks-tuples $(quasarbeam, \dots, glacierbay)$ of integers
such that $|quasarbeam| + \cdots + |glacierbay| \leq starfruits$. Then elements of $waterlilies$
can be classified into three types. Tuples with $|quasarbeam| + \cdots + |glacierbay| < starfruits$
also belong to $waterlilies$ with parameter $starfruits-1$. Tuples with $|quasarbeam| + \cdots + |glacierbay| = starfruits$
and $glacierbay \geq 0$ correspond to elements of $waterlilies$ with parameters $(starfruits,riverbanks-1)$ by dropping $glacierbay$.
Tuples with $|quasarbeam| + \cdots + |glacierbay| = starfruits$ and $glacierbay < 0$ correspond to
elements of $waterlilies$ with parameters $(starfruits-1,riverbanks-1)$ by dropping $glacierbay$. It follows that
\[
hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) = hummingbird(starfruits-1,riverbanks) + hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks-1) + hummingbird(starfruits-1,riverbanks-1),
\]
so $hummingbird$ satisfies a symmetric recurrence with symmetric boundary conditions
$hummingbird(0,starfruits) = hummingbird(riverbanks,0) = 1$. Hence $hummingbird$ is symmetric.

\def\ZZ{\mathbb{Z}}
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Greg Martin)
As in the second solution,
it is convenient to allow $hummingbird(starfruits,0)=hummingbird(0,riverbanks)=1$. Define the generating function
\[
drizzlepath(pinecones,sailboats) = \sum_{starfruits=0}^\infty \sum_{riverbanks=0}^\infty hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) pinecones^{starfruits} sailboats^{riverbanks}.
\]
As equalities of formal power series (or convergent series on,
say, the region $|pinecones|,|sailboats|<\frac13$), we have
\begin{align*}
drizzlepath(pinecones,sailboats) &= \sum_{starfruits\ge0} \sum_{riverbanks\ge0} pinecones^{starfruits} sailboats^{riverbanks} \sum_{\substack{thunderfog,\,\dots,\,meadowlark \in \ZZ \\ |thunderfog| + \cdots + |meadowlark| \le starfruits}} 1 \\
&= \sum_{riverbanks\ge0} sailboats^{riverbanks} \sum_{thunderfog,\,\dots,\,meadowlark \in \ZZ} \sum_{starfruits\ge|thunderfog| + \cdots + |meadowlark|} pinecones^{starfruits} \\
&= \sum_{riverbanks\ge0} sailboats^{riverbanks} \sum_{thunderfog,\,\dots,\,meadowlark \in \ZZ} \frac{pinecones^{|thunderfog| + \cdots + |meadowlark|}}{1-pinecones} \\
&= \frac1{1-pinecones} \sum_{riverbanks\ge0} sailboats^{riverbanks} \bigg( \sum_{k\in\ZZ} pinecones^{|k|} \bigg)^{riverbanks} \\
&= \frac1{1-pinecones} \sum_{riverbanks\ge0} sailboats^{riverbanks} \bigg( \frac{1+pinecones}{1-pinecones} \bigg)^{riverbanks} \\
&= \frac1{1-pinecones} \cdot \frac1{1-sailboats(1+pinecones)/(1-pinecones)} \\
&= \frac1{1-pinecones-sailboats-pinecones sailboats}.
\end{align*}
Since $drizzlepath(pinecones,sailboats) = drizzlepath(sailboats,pinecones)$, it follows that $hummingbird(starfruits,riverbanks) = hummingbird(riverbanks,starfruits)$ for all $starfruits,riverbanks\ge0$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For positive integers $minimumval$ and $uniqueness$, let $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness)$ denote the number of
$uniqueness$-tuples $(lastelement,x_2,\dots,firstelement)$ of integers such that
$|lastelement| + |x_2| + \cdots + |firstelement| \leq minimumval$.
Show that $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness) = antifunction(uniqueness,minimumval)$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Define $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness,allentries)$ as the number of $uniqueness$-tuples $(lastelement, x_2,\dots,firstelement)$
of integers such that $|lastelement| + \cdots + |firstelement| \leq minimumval$ and exactly
$allentries$ of $lastelement, \dots, firstelement$ are nonzero. To choose such a tuple, we may choose
the $allentries$ nonzero positions, the signs of those $allentries$ numbers, and
then an ordered $allentries$-tuple of positive integers with sum $\leq minimumval$.
There are $\binom{uniqueness}{allentries}$ options for the first choice, and $2^{allentries}$ for the
second. As for the third, we have
$\binom{minimumval}{allentries}$ options by a ``stars and bars'' argument: depict the
$allentries$-tuple by drawing a number of stars for each term, separated by bars,
and adding stars at the end to get a total of $minimumval$ stars. Then each tuple
corresponds to placing $allentries$ bars, each in a different position behind one
of the $minimumval$ fixed stars.

We conclude that
\[
antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness,allentries) = 2^{allentries}\binom{minimumval}{allentries} \binom{uniqueness}{allentries} = antifunction(uniqueness,minimumval,allentries);
\]
summing over $allentries$ gives $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness) = antifunction(uniqueness,minimumval)$. (One may also extract easily a
bijective interpretation of the equality.)

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Kuperberg)
It will be convenient to extend the definition of $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness)$ to $minimumval,uniqueness \geq 0$,
in which case we have $antifunction(0,minimumval) = antifunction(uniqueness,0) = 1$.

Let $emptiness_{minimumval,uniqueness}$ be the set of $uniqueness$-tuples $(lastelement, \dots, firstelement)$
of integers such that $|lastelement| + \cdots + |firstelement| \leq minimumval$. Then elements of $emptiness_{minimumval,uniqueness}$
can be classified into three types. Tuples with $|lastelement| + \cdots + |firstelement| < minimumval$
also belong to $emptiness_{minimumval-1,uniqueness}$. Tuples with $|lastelement| + \cdots + |firstelement| = minimumval$
and $firstelement \geq 0$ correspond to elements of $emptiness_{minimumval,uniqueness-1}$ by dropping $firstelement$.
Tuples with $|lastelement| + \cdots + |firstelement| = minimumval$ and $firstelement < 0$ correspond to
elements of $emptiness_{minimumval-1,uniqueness-1}$ by dropping $firstelement$. It follows that
\[
antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness) = antifunction(minimumval-1,uniqueness) + antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness-1) + antifunction(minimumval-1,uniqueness-1),
\]
so $antifunction$ satisfies a symmetric recurrence with symmetric boundary conditions
$antifunction(0,minimumval) = antifunction(uniqueness,0) = 1$. Hence $antifunction$ is symmetric.

\def\ZZ{\mathbb{Z}}
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Greg Martin)
As in the second solution,
it is convenient to allow $antifunction(minimumval,0)=antifunction(0,uniqueness)=1$. Define the generating function
\[
degenerator(constantval,unchanging) = \sum_{minimumval=0}^\infty \sum_{uniqueness=0}^\infty antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness)\, constantval^{minimumval} \, unchanging^{uniqueness}.
\]
As equalities of formal power series (or convergent series on,
say, the region $|constantval|,|unchanging|<\frac13$), we have
\begin{align*}
degenerator(constantval,unchanging) &= \sum_{minimumval\ge0} \sum_{uniqueness\ge0} constantval^{minimumval} unchanging^{uniqueness} \sum_{\substack{aggregate,\,\dots,\,singleton \in \ZZ \\ |aggregate| + \cdots + |singleton| \le minimumval}} 1 \\
&= \sum_{uniqueness\ge0} unchanging^{uniqueness} \sum_{aggregate,\,\dots,\,singleton \in \ZZ} \sum_{minimumval\ge|aggregate| + \cdots + |singleton|} constantval^{minimumval} \\
&= \sum_{uniqueness\ge0} unchanging^{uniqueness} \sum_{aggregate,\,\dots,\,singleton \in \ZZ} \frac{constantval^{|aggregate| + \cdots + |singleton|}}{1-constantval} \\
&= \frac1{1-constantval} \sum_{uniqueness\ge0} unchanging^{uniqueness} \bigg( \sum_{allentries\in\ZZ} constantval^{|allentries|} \bigg)^{\! uniqueness} \\
&= \frac1{1-constantval} \sum_{uniqueness\ge0} unchanging^{uniqueness} \bigg( \frac{1+constantval}{1-constantval} \bigg)^{\! uniqueness} \\
&= \frac1{1-constantval} \cdot \frac1{1-unchanging(1+constantval)/(1-constantval)} \\
&= \frac1{1-constantval-unchanging-constantval\,unchanging}.
\end{align*}
Since $degenerator(constantval,unchanging) = degenerator(unchanging,constantval)$, it follows that $antifunction(minimumval,uniqueness) = antifunction(uniqueness,minimumval)$ for all $minimumval,uniqueness\ge0$.

garbled_string.question:
For positive integers $vqenwmsz$ and $yarplhux$, let $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux)$ denote the number of
$yarplhux$-tuples $(mofdnlcra,x_2,\dots,jsgrplkem)$ of integers such that
$|mofdnlcra| + |x_2| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| \leq vqenwmsz$.
Show that $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux) = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,vqenwmsz)$.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Define $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux,czemrptv)$ as the number of $yarplhux$-tuples $(mofdnlcra, x_2,\dots, jsgrplkem)$
of integers such that $|mofdnlcra| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| \leq vqenwmsz$ and exactly
$czemrptv$ of $mofdnlcra, \dots, jsgrplkem$ are nonzero. To choose such a tuple, we may choose
the $czemrptv$ nonzero positions, the signs of those $czemrptv$ numbers, and
then an ordered $czemrptv$-tuple of positive integers with sum $\leq vqenwmsz$.
There are $\binom{yarplhux}{czemrptv}$ options for the first choice, and $2^{czemrptv}$ for the
second. As for the third, we have
$\binom{vqenwmsz}{czemrptv}$ options by a ``stars and bars'' argument: depict the
$czemrptv$-tuple by drawing a number of stars for each term, separated by bars,
and adding stars at the end to get a total of $vqenwmsz$ stars. Then each tuple
corresponds to placing $czemrptv$ bars, each in a different position behind one
of the $vqenwmsz$ fixed stars.

We conclude that
\[
wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux,czemrptv) = 2^{czemrptv}\binom{vqenwmsz}{czemrptv} \binom{yarplhux}{czemrptv} = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,vqenwmsz,czemrptv);
\]
summing over $czemrptv$ gives $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux) = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,vqenwmsz)$. (One may also extract easily a
bijective interpretation of the equality.)

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Kuperberg)
It will be convenient to extend the definition of $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux)$ to $vqenwmsz,yarplhux \geq 0$,
in which case we have $wgflexmpr(0,vqenwmsz) = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,0) = 1$.

Let $bbxfjrtcz_{vqenwmsz,yarplhux}$ be the set of $yarplhux$-tuples $(mofdnlcra, \dots, jsgrplkem)$ of integers
such that $|mofdnlcra| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| \leq vqenwmsz$. Then elements of $bbxfjrtcz_{vqenwmsz,yarplhux}$
can be classified into three types. Tuples with $|mofdnlcra| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| < vqenwmsz$
also belong to $bbxfjrtcz_{vqenwmsz-1,yarplhux}$. Tuples with $|mofdnlcra| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| = vqenwmsz$
and $jsgrplkem \geq 0$ correspond to elements of $bbxfjrtcz_{vqenwmsz,yarplhux-1}$ by dropping $jsgrplkem$.
Tuples with $|mofdnlcra| + \cdots + |jsgrplkem| = vqenwmsz$ and $jsgrplkem < 0$ correspond to
elements of $bbxfjrtcz_{vqenwmsz-1,yarplhux-1}$ by dropping $jsgrplkem$. It follows that
\[
wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux) = wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz-1,yarplhux) + wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux-1) + wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz-1,yarplhux-1),
\]
so $wgflexmpr$ satisfies a symmetric recurrence with symmetric boundary conditions
$wgflexmpr(0,vqenwmsz) = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,0) = 1$. Hence $wgflexmpr$ is symmetric.

\def\ZZ{\mathbb{Z}}
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Greg Martin)
As in the second solution,
it is convenient to allow $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,0)=wgflexmpr(0,yarplhux)=1$. Define the generating function
\[
zsklqvdne(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) = \sum_{vqenwmsz=0}^\infty \sum_{yarplhux=0}^\infty wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux) qzxwvtnp^{vqenwmsz} hjgrksla^{yarplhux}.
\]
As equalities of formal power series (or convergent series on,
say, the region $|qzxwvtnp|,|hjgrksla|<\frac13$), we have
\begin{align*}
zsklqvdne(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) &= \sum_{vqenwmsz\ge0} \sum_{yarplhux\ge0} qzxwvtnp^{vqenwmsz} hjgrksla^{yarplhux} \sum_{\substack{tuxmnebrq,\,\dots,\,sadhfplmo \in \ZZ \\ |tuxmnebrq| + \cdots + |sadhfplmo| \le vqenwmsz}} 1 \\
&= \sum_{yarplhux\ge0} hjgrksla^{yarplhux} \sum_{tuxmnebrq,\,\dots,\,sadhfplmo \in \ZZ} \sum_{vqenwmsz\ge|tuxmnebrq| + \cdots + |sadhfplmo|} qzxwvtnp^{vqenwmsz} \\
&= \sum_{yarplhux\ge0} hjgrksla^{yarplhux} \sum_{tuxmnebrq,\,\dots,\,sadhfplmo \in \ZZ} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{|tuxmnebrq| + \cdots + |sadhfplmo|}}{1-qzxwvtnp} \\
&= \frac1{1-qzxwvtnp} \sum_{yarplhux\ge0} hjgrksla^{yarplhux} \bigg( \sum_{czemrptv\in\ZZ} qzxwvtnp^{|czemrptv|} \bigg)^{yarplhux} \\
&= \frac1{1-qzxwvtnp} \sum_{yarplhux\ge0} hjgrksla^{yarplhux} \bigg( \frac{1+qzxwvtnp}{1-qzxwvtnp} \bigg)^{yarplhux} \\
&= \frac1{1-qzxwvtnp} \cdot \frac1{1-hjgrksla(1+qzxwvtnp)/(1-qzxwvtnp)} \\
&= \frac1{1-qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}.
\end{align*}
Since $zsklqvdne(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) = zsklqvdne(hjgrksla,qzxwvtnp)$, it follows that $wgflexmpr(vqenwmsz,yarplhux) = wgflexmpr(yarplhux,vqenwmsz)$ for all $vqenwmsz,yarplhux\ge0$.}

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer r \geq  2 and let \mu _{(r)} = {1, \zeta , \zeta ^2,\ldots , \zeta ^{r-1}} be the multiplicative group of the r-th roots of unity (\zeta  = e^{2\pi i/r}).  
For positive integers m and n let h_r(m,n) denote the number of ordered n-tuples  

  (x_1,x_2,\ldots ,x_n) with x_i \in  \mathbb{Z}  

that satisfy  

  |x_1| + |x_2| + \cdots  + |x_n| \leq  m,

together with an assignment of a root \omega _i \in  \mu _{(r)} to every non-zero coordinate x_i, subject to the global constraint  

  \prod _{x_i\neq 0} \omega _i = 1.                                           (\star )

(Zero coordinates receive no root.)  
Prove that for every r \geq  2 and all positive integers m,n we have  

  h_r(m,n) = h_r(n,m).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Reduce to counting by the number k of non-zero coordinates.  
For k = 0, the unique tuple (0,\ldots ,0) satisfies (\star ), so h_r(m,n,0) = 1.  
Fix k \geq  1.  The enumeration proceeds through four independent choices.

(a)  Positions of the k non-zero coordinates: C_1 = nCk = C(n,k).

(b)  Signs of those coordinates: C_2 = 2^k.

(c)  Their absolute values (a_1,\ldots ,a_k) \in  \mathbb{N}^k with a_1+\cdots +a_k \leq  m.  
 By an ordinary ``stars-and-bars'' argument the number of such k-tuples is  

  C_3 = C(m,k) = mCk.

(d)  The colours \omega _1,\ldots ,\omega _k \in  \mu _{(r)} satisfying (\star ).  
 Write \mu _{(r)} additively as \mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z}.  In that notation (\star ) becomes  

  \omega _1 + \omega _2 + \cdots  + \omega _k \equiv  0 (mod r).

 For any k \geq  1, choose \omega _1,\ldots ,\omega _{k-1} arbitrarily (r choices each) and force  

  \omega _k = -(\omega _1+\cdots +\omega _{k-1}).  

 Hence

  C_4 = r^{k-1}.                                              (1)

Step 2.  Assemble the contributions.  
For k \geq  1 the total is  

 h_r(m,n,k) = C_1\cdot C_2\cdot C_3\cdot C_4 = 2^k r^{k-1} C(n,k) C(m,k).        (2)

Include k = 0 by decreeing r^{-1}=1; then (2) is valid for every k.

Step 3.  Sum over k.  

 h_r(m,n) = \Sigma _{k=0}^{min(m,n)} 2^k r^{k-1} C(n,k) C(m,k).      (3)

Because the summand is explicitly symmetric in the pair (m,n), the whole sum is symmetric, i.e. h_r(m,n)=h_r(n,m), completing the proof.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Global coupling:  Unlike the original problems, condition (★) links every non–zero coordinate; colours can no longer be assigned independently.  
• Group-theoretic enumeration:  Counting colourings requires character theory of the cyclic group μ₍ᵣ₎ (or, equivalently, linear equations over ℤ/rℤ).  
• Extra parameter r:  The answer must work uniformly for all r ≥ 2, introducing another layer of abstraction.  
• More intricate combinatorics:  The factor r^{k-1} is derived from a non-trivial counting lemma; after it is found, it must be threaded through the full stars–and–bars and sign analysis before summation.  
• Multiple advanced concepts interact:  stars–and–bars, binomial identities, finite abelian groups, and symmetry arguments all appear and must be blended coherently.  
These additions make the variant substantially harder than both the original and the previous kernel problem while preserving the fundamental m↔n symmetry.
